sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Corrosive agents are substances that cause tissue damage upon contact. Ingestion, inhalation, or dermal exposure to these agents can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. Corrosive agents are chemicals (acids or alkalis) that can cause immediate and progressive damage to tissues on contact. Contamination can occur as a result of accidental occupational exposure or deliberate exposure/ingestion for self-harm.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ingestion
- Intra oral, oesophageal and/or abdominal pain, burns and/or ulcerations
- Drooling, dysphagia
- Nausea/vomiting
- Risk of perforation and sepsis
- Inhaled
- Dyspnoea, stridor, wheeze, cough, hoarse voice, pulmonary oedema
- Topical Exposure
- Localised pain, burning, blistering, numbness
- Skin discolouration (redness, severe cases may blacken or char skin), rash, cracked, dry or peeling skin, swelling
- Eye exposure
- Burning, stinging, redness, visual disturbance/loss, orbital swelling, lacrimation

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not approach an unsafe scene
- One or more patients may indicate a hazardous atmosphere. Do not approach until the scene is declared safe by the ESO/ERT after gas monitoring has been conducted
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- The absence of lip or oral burns does not exclude significant gastro-oesophageal injury
- The extent of a direct chemical injury depends on the pH, concentration and volume
- In cases of ingestion, do not induce or encourage vomiting
- Oxygen administration should be withheld in paraquat toxicity unless patient is hypoxic.
Aim for 88%-92% saturation

- All patients with exposure to hydrofluoric acid must be transferred to hospital and not managed on site passed initial treatment. This applies to both inhalation and dermal exposure due to dermal injury ranging from nil visible injury to full thickness burns and must be observed/treated in hospital

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance
- Position patient appropriately
- Consider Oxygen if indicated
- IPPV if required
- Remove any clothing and constrictive jewellery
- Consider Diphoterine if available and close by (not for use on hydrofluoric acid)
- Initiate irrigation of the affected area for minimum 20 minutes. Continue during transport
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetics
- Consider IV analgesia in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider calcium gluconate gel/solution/nebuliser depending on exposure route of hydrofluoric acid
- Consider prophylactic antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
